subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! Look around you right now and you are surrounded by 3D shapes. The book on the table is a cuboid. The tin of tomatoes in the kitchen is a cylinder. A die from a board game is a cube, and a ball for football is a sphere. Everything you can pick up and hold, everything that takes up space and casts a shadow, is a three-dimensional shape. In this unit you will learn to look at these solid objects the way a mathematician does – to name them, to describe their parts exactly, and to understand how they are built.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to explain a step in a new way, give you extra practice, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky. The most helpful thing of all in this unit is to have real objects beside you – a box, a tin, a die, a ball. Pick them up, turn them over, and count their parts with your own hands.

Why does this matter? When Yusuf helps his family pack goods into boxes at the Hargeisa market, he is thinking about how solid shapes fit together. When Khadija folds a flat sheet of card into a small box for dates at Eid, she is using a net. When an architect in Mogadishu designs a building, or an engineer plans a water tank, they must understand faces, edges and volume. Spatial thinking – the ability to picture and turn shapes in your mind – is one of the most valuable skills you can build, and it starts right here.
Explain the difference between 2D and 3D shapes and give real examples of each.
Name common 3D shapes: cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere, prism and pyramid.
Identify and count the faces, edges and vertices of a 3D shape.
Describe the special properties of a cube (6 square faces, 12 equal edges, 8 vertices).
Understand what a net is and identify which nets fold into a cube.
Draw 3D shapes on isometric paper and show which faces are visible and which are hidden.
Recognise that the same shape looks different from different viewpoints.
Find the volume of a cube or cuboid by counting or multiplying unit cubes.

concepts.title: What Makes a Shape 3D?
concepts.explanation: A 2D shape, or two-dimensional shape, is completely flat. Think of a square drawn on paper. It has length (how long it is) and width (how wide it is), but no thickness at all. You can only ever see it from one side, because there is no other side to see. Circles, triangles, rectangles and pentagons are all 2D shapes. They live on the flat surface of the page. A 3D shape, or three-dimensional shape, is solid. It has length and width like a 2D shape, but it also has a third measurement: height or depth. This third…
concepts.example: Here is a simple way to feel the difference. Draw a square on paper: that is 2D. Now pick up a die: that is a cube, the 3D version of a square. The square has become a solid box with a front, a back, a top, a bottom and…

concepts.title: Faces, Edges and Vertices
concepts.explanation: Every 3D shape is built from three kinds of parts, and a mathematician can describe any solid by counting them. These three parts are faces, edges and vertices, and they are the vocabulary you will use for the rest of this unit. A face is a flat surface of the shape – like one whole side of a box. Faces are the parts you could paint.
concepts.example: An edge is a straight line where two faces meet – like the sharp line along the side of a book.

concepts.title: The Cube in Detail
concepts.explanation: The cube is the most important 3D shape to know well, so we will study it closely. A cube is a special solid where every face is an identical square. Because all six faces are the same square, a cube looks the same whichever way you turn it – that is what makes it so neat and useful. The full list of cube properties is worth learning by heart:
concepts.example: It has 6 faces, and every single one is a square of the same size.

concepts.title: A Family of 3D Shapes
concepts.explanation: The cube is just one member of a whole family of solids. Let us meet the others, because each has its own faces, edges and vertices, and its own uses in the world. A cuboid, also called a rectangular prism, is like a cube that has been stretched. Its 6 faces are rectangles rather than squares, and opposite faces match each other. A cuboid has 6 faces, 12 edges and 8 vertices – the same counts as a cube, but the faces need not be equal. Books, smartphones, cereal boxes and bricks are all cuboids. In fact, a cube is…
concepts.example: A triangular prism has two triangular faces at its ends and three rectangular faces along its sides. It has 5 faces, 9 edges and 6 vertices. You have seen this shape in a Toblerone chocolate bar, in the ridge of a tent,…

concepts.title: Nets – Unfolding a Solid
concepts.explanation: A net is a flat pattern that you can cut out and fold up to make a 3D shape. Here is the best way to picture it: imagine carefully unfolding a cardboard box until it lies completely flat on the table. That flat pattern of joined-up faces is the net of the box. Fold it back along the creases and it becomes a solid again. Every 3D shape with flat faces can be opened out into a net. Nets matter because they show the link between the flat 2D world and the solid 3D world. The number of shapes in a net tells you the…
concepts.example: But here is the important catch: not every arrangement of 6 squares will fold into a cube. The squares must be joined in the right way, so that when you fold along the edges each square becomes a different face with…

concepts.title: Drawing Solids and Seeing Different Views
concepts.explanation: Drawing a solid shape on flat paper is a real challenge, because you must create the illusion that the shape has depth when the paper itself is flat. This is exactly what artists, architects and designers do every day, and there is a clever tool that makes it much easier: isometric paper. Isometric paper is covered with dots or lines arranged in a triangular pattern. Ordinary squared paper helps you draw flat squares and rectangles; isometric paper helps you draw cubes and cuboids that look solid. To draw a cube…
concepts.example: When you look at a solid shape, you can only ever see some of its parts at once; the rest are hidden round the back. For a cube sitting on a table, you can usually see three faces at a time – perhaps the top, the front…

methods.title: How to count faces, edges and vertices
methods.example: A triangular prism sits on the table. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have?
steps: Count the faces in a fixed order: top, bottom, then around the sides.
Count the edges the same careful way, including the hidden ones.
Count the vertices (corner points) last.
Check with Euler's rule: V − E + F should equal 2.

methods.title: How to find the volume of a cuboid
methods.example: Amina counts a square-based pyramid and says it has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. Is her counting right?
steps: Write down the length, the width and the height.
Multiply length × width to find the cubes in one layer.
Multiply that by the height to count all the layers.
Give the answer in cubic units (cm³ or m³).

methods.title: How to test a cube net
methods.example: Amina counts a square-based pyramid and says it has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. Is her counting right?
steps: Count the squares – a cube net needs exactly 6.
Imagine folding: four squares curl into the sides, one is the top, one is the bottom.
Check that no two squares would overlap and none is missing.
When unsure, cut it out and fold it to be certain.

methods.title: Hidden faces
methods.example: Omar looks at a cube and can see the top face, the front face and the right side face. Which faces are hidden?
steps: Solution: A cube has 6 faces.
If Omar can see the top, front and right, then the hidden faces are the ones directly opposite each of those: the bottom, the back and the left side.
You can never see more than 3 faces of a cube at once, and each visible face hides the one opposite it.

methods.title: Volume of a cuboid
methods.example: A cuboid box is 5 cm long, 4 cm wide and 2 cm high. What is its volume?
steps: Solution: Use volume = length × width × height.
That is 5 × 4 × 2.
Work it in steps: 5 × 4 = 20, and 20 × 2 = 40.
The volume is 40 cm³.

methods.title: Packing small cubes
methods.example: How many small cubes with 2 cm edges fit inside a large cube with 6 cm edges?
steps: Solution: Along one edge, 6 ÷ 2 = 3 small cubes fit.
The same is true along all three directions, so the box holds 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 small cubes.
This is the same as finding how many unit blocks build the big shape.

workedExamples.title: Count faces, edges and vertices
workedExamples.prompt: A triangular prism sits on the table. How many faces, edges and vertices does it have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count in order. Faces: the two triangular ends make 2, and the three rectangular sides make 3, giving 2 + 3 = 5 faces. Edges: each triangle has 3 edges (2 triangles = 6) plus 3 edges joining the ends, giving 6 + 3 = 9 edges. Vertices: each triangular end has 3 corners, and 3 + 3 = 6 vertices. Check with Euler: 6 − 9 + 5 = 2. Correct!

workedExamples.title: Use Euler's rule to check
workedExamples.prompt: Amina counts a square-based pyramid and says it has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. Is her counting right?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Test with Euler's rule, V − E + F. That is 5 − 8 + 5 = 2. Because the answer is 2, her counting is consistent and correct. A square pyramid does indeed have 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. Worked Example 3: Which net folds into a cube? A net is made of four squares in a row, with one extra square above the first square and one extra square below the third square. Will it fold into a cube? Solution: First, count the squares: 4 + 1 + 1 = 6 squares, which is the right number for a cube. Then imagine…

workedExamples.title: Which net folds into a cube?
workedExamples.prompt: Amina counts a square-based pyramid and says it has 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. Is her counting right?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Test with Euler's rule, V − E + F. That is 5 − 8 + 5 = 2. Because the answer is 2, her counting is consistent and correct. A square pyramid does indeed have 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices. Worked Example 3: Which net folds into a cube? A net is made of four squares in a row, with one extra square above the first square and one extra square below the third square. Will it fold into a cube? Solution: First, count the squares: 4 + 1 + 1 = 6 squares, which is the right number for a cube. Then imagine…

workedExamples.title: Hidden faces
workedExamples.prompt: Omar looks at a cube and can see the top face, the front face and the right side face. Which faces are hidden?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A cube has 6 faces. If Omar can see the top, front and right, then the hidden faces are the ones directly opposite each of those: the bottom, the back and the left side. You can never see more than 3 faces of a cube at once, and each visible face hides the one opposite it.

workedExamples.title: Volume of a cuboid
workedExamples.prompt: A cuboid box is 5 cm long, 4 cm wide and 2 cm high. What is its volume?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use volume = length × width × height. That is 5 × 4 × 2. Work it in steps: 5 × 4 = 20, and 20 × 2 = 40. The volume is 40 cm³. You can picture it as two layers, each holding 5 × 4 = 20 unit cubes.

workedExamples.title: Packing small cubes
workedExamples.prompt: How many small cubes with 2 cm edges fit inside a large cube with 6 cm edges?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Along one edge, 6 ÷ 2 = 3 small cubes fit. The same is true along all three directions, so the box holds 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 small cubes. This is the same as finding how many unit blocks build the big shape.

workedExamples.title: Different views
workedExamples.prompt: Zahra looks at a cylinder (a tin of milk). What 2D shape does she see from the side, and what does she see from directly above?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: From the side, the curved surface appears flattened into a rectangle. From directly above, looking down on the round lid, she sees a circle. The same solid gives two very different flat views, which is why more than one view is needed to describe a shape fully.

workedExamples.title: Total edge length of a cube
workedExamples.prompt: A cube has edges that are 7 cm long. What is the total length of all its edges added together?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A cube has 12 equal edges. If each is 7 cm, the total is 12 × 7 = 84 cm. Because every edge of a cube is the same length, one multiplication does the whole job.

workedExamples.title: What 2D shape do you see from
workedExamples.prompt: What 2D shape do you see from: a) a cube from above b) a cylinder from the side c) a cylinder from the end?
workedExamples.solution: a) square, b) rectangle, c) circle.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: How many faces of a cube can you see at one time? How many are hidden?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Use Euler's rule to check: a triangular prism has 6 vertices, 9 edges and 5 faces. Does V − E + F = 2?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A large cube of edge 5 cm is built from 1 cm unit cubes. How many unit cubes are used?

activities.title: 3D Shape Treasure Hunt
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your eyes.
steps: Walk through your home and find one real example of each shape: cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere.
For each object, draw a quick picture and write where you found it.
Count and record its faces, edges and vertices.
Make a chart sorting all your objects by shape. Which shape did you find most often?
Keep your chart – it shows how common 3D shapes are in everyday life.

activities.title: Net Construction Workshop
activities.materials: squared paper or card, scissors, tape, colouring pencils.
steps: Draw the cross-shaped cube net: a row of four squares with one square above and one below the second square.
Cut it out carefully and fold along every line to make a closed cube. Tape the edges.
Now design two more nets of 6 squares that you think will fold into a cube, and test them.
Record which nets worked and which did not. For the ones that failed, explain why.
There are eleven different nets that fold into a cube – see how many you can discover!

activities.title: Build a Cube from Unit Cubes (Volume Game)
activities.materials: small identical cubes – dice, sugar cubes, or paper cubes you make.
steps: Build one flat layer that is 3 cubes long and 2 cubes wide. Count the cubes in the layer.
Stack a second and third identical layer on top. Count the total number of cubes.
Check your count using the rule length × width × height.
Challenge a family member: build a secret cuboid and ask them to work out its volume.

activities.title: Different Views Investigation
activities.materials: a tin, a box, and a ball.
steps: Place the tin on the table. Draw what it looks like from the side, then from directly above.
Do the same for the box: draw the front view, the side view and the top view.
Ask someone to hide an object and describe only its views – can you guess the shape?
Talk about why an architect needs more than one view to describe a building.

activities.title: Shape Property Quiz Game (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family or friends, and small slips of paper.
steps: On each slip, write a shape clue such as 'I have 6 square faces' or 'I have no flat faces'.
Take turns drawing a slip and naming the shape it describes.
Score a point for each correct answer. The clues can mention faces, edges or vertices.
Make the game harder by adding clues about nets and volume.

activities.title: Design and Build a Box
activities.materials: card, a ruler, scissors and tape.
steps: Decide on a small object to hold, such as a gift of dates for Eid.
Draw the net of a cuboid box big enough to hold it, marking the length, width and height.
Cut out and fold your net, then tape it into a box and check the object fits.
Work out the volume of your box using length × width × height. Decorate it if you like.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which 3D shape do you meet most often in daily life? What was the trickiest part of folding a net? How did building with unit cubes help you understand volume? Which activity taught you the most about faces, edges and vertices? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Apply Euler's rule (Vertices − Edges + Faces = 2) to check your counting.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: There is a beautiful pattern that connects these three numbers, discovered by a mathematician named Euler (say it 'Oiler'). For any solid with flat faces, if you take the number of vertices, subtract the number of edges, and add the number of faces, you always get 2. We write it as V − E + F = 2. For a cube: 8 − 12 + 6 = 2. It works! This little rule is a wonderful way to check your counting: if your three numbers do not give 2, you have miscounted somewhere and should count again.

rules.title: Key idea 3
rules.text: Because counting layer by layer always comes down to the same multiplication, we can write a rule. The volume of a cuboid = length × width × height. For our example, 4 × 3 × 3 = 36 cm³, exactly matching the cubes we counted. A cube is just a cuboid whose length, width and height are all equal, so the volume of a cube is one edge multiplied by itself three times. A cube of edge 2 cm has a volume of 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 cm³.

rules.title: Key idea 4
rules.text: Worked Example 2: Use Euler's rule to check

rules.title: Key idea 5
rules.text: Solution: Test with Euler's rule, V − E + F. That is 5 − 8 + 5 = 2. Because the answer is 2, her counting is consistent and correct. A square pyramid does indeed have 5 faces, 8 edges and 5 vertices.

rules.title: Key idea 6
rules.text: Use Euler's rule (V − E + F = 2) to check whether a shape with 8 vertices, 12 edges and 6 faces is possible. Which shape is it?

terms: A flat surface of a 3D shape
A line where two faces meet
A corner point where edges meet
Same shape all along its length
A base with triangular faces meeting at an apex
A flat pattern that folds into a solid
The space a solid fills, in cubic units
commonMistakes: A cube is solid with 6 square faces; a square is flat
Count in a fixed order and check with Euler's rule
Only 11 arrangements fold; cut and fold to test

realProblems.prompt: A shopkeeper in Eastleigh packs 8 small sugar cubes into the smallest possible cubic box. What arrangement of cubes does she use, and what is the box's edge in cubes?
realProblems.answer: 1) A 2 × 2 × 2 arrangement, so the box is 2 cubes along each edge.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) A 2 × 2 × 2 arrangement, so the box is 2 cubes along each edge.

realProblems.prompt: A water tank shaped like a cuboid is 4 m long, 3 m wide and 2 m high. How many cubic metres of water can it hold when full?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Maryam designs a box with a net of 6 rectangles. What shape is the box, and how many vertices does it have?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A hollow cube measures 3 cubes along each edge. How many unit cubes are on the outside if the single centre cube is the only one hidden inside?
realProblems.answer: A 3 × 3 × 3 cube has 27 unit cubes; if only the centre is hidden, 27 − 1 = 26 are on the outside.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A 3 × 3 × 3 cube has 27 unit cubes; if only the centre is hidden, 27 − 1 = 26 are on the outside.

realProblems.prompt: Ibrahim looks at a triangular prism (a tent). Draw or describe what he sees from the front, the side and the top.
realProblems.answer: Front = triangle, side = rectangle, top = rectangle.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Front = triangle, side = rectangle, top = rectangle.

realProblems.prompt: A large cube of edge 5 cm is built from 1 cm unit cubes. How many unit cubes are used?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A large cube of edge 5 cm is built from 1 cm unit cubes. How many unit cubes are used?
How many small 3 cm cubes fit inside a box that is 9 cm × 9 cm × 9 cm?
A net has 5 squares. Can it fold into a closed cube? Explain your answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: No – a closed cube needs exactly 6 faces, so 5 squares can only make an open cube.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: You stack 3 identical cubes in a tower. How many square faces are completely hidden where cubes touch, and how many are visible?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 hidden faces (2 touching joins × 2 faces each); total faces 3 × 6 = 18, so 18 − 4 = 14 visible.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Nets – Unfolding a Solid.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A net is a flat pattern that you can cut out and fold up to make a 3D shape. Here is the best way to picture it: imagine carefully unfolding a cardboard box until it lies completely flat on the table. That flat pattern of joined-up faces is the net of the box. Fold it back along the creases and it becomes a solid again. Every 3D shape with flat faces can be opened out into a net. Nets matter because they show the link between the flat 2D world and the solid 3D world. The number of shapes in a net tells you the…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Drawing Solids and Seeing Different Views.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Drawing a solid shape on flat paper is a real challenge, because you must create the illusion that the shape has depth when the paper itself is flat. This is exactly what artists, architects and designers do every day, and there is a clever tool that makes it much easier: isometric paper. Isometric paper is covered with dots or lines arranged in a triangular pattern. Ordinary squared paper helps you draw flat squares and rectangles; isometric paper helps you draw cubes and cuboids that look solid. To draw a cube…

questions.question: What does “Face” mean?
options: A flat surface of a 3D shape
A line where two faces meet
A corner point where edges meet
questions.answer: A flat surface of a 3D shape
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Face means A flat surface of a 3D shape.

questions.question: What does “Edge” mean?
options: A line where two faces meet
A corner point where edges meet
Same shape all along its length
questions.answer: A line where two faces meet
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Edge means A line where two faces meet.

questions.question: What does “Vertex” mean?
options: A corner point where edges meet
Same shape all along its length
A base with triangular faces meeting at an apex
questions.answer: A corner point where edges meet
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Vertex means A corner point where edges meet.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to explain a step in a new way, give you extra practice, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky. The most helpful thing of all in this unit is to have real objects beside you – a box, a tin, a die, a ball. Pick them up, turn them over, and count their parts with your own hands.

I can why does this matter? When Yusuf helps his family pack goods into boxes at the Hargeisa market, he is thinking about how solid shapes fit together. When Khadija folds a flat sheet of card into a small box for dates at Eid, she is using a net. When an architect in Mogadishu designs a building, or an engineer plans a water tank, they must understand faces, edges and volume. Spatial thinking – the ability to picture and turn shapes in your mind – is one of the most valuable skills you can build, and it starts right here.
I can explain the difference between 2D and 3D shapes and give real examples of each.
I can name common 3D shapes: cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere, prism and pyramid.
I can identify and count the faces, edges and vertices of a 3D shape.
I can describe the special properties of a cube (6 square faces, 12 equal edges, 8 vertices).
I can understand what a net is and identify which nets fold into a cube.
I can draw 3D shapes on isometric paper and show which faces are visible and which are hidden.
